envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Clay Building Material and Refractories Manufacturing

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat industrial supply and relining orders for consumable refractories, plus distributor, builder, architect-specified, and project orders for brick, tile, and other building materials; revenue is usually per ton, unit, pallet, lot, or project.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal plants, shells, raw clay mining or beneficiation, pottery and plumbing fixtures, glass products, cement, concrete products, and installation-only contractors classified outside NAICS 327120.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms repeatedly manufacturing and supplying clay and nonclay refractories, refractory cement and mortar, ceramic and structural clay tile, brick, roofing and paving clay products, flue linings, and related fired structural-clay materials to external industrial, construction, distribution, and government customers.

a.bridge: BLS publishes occupation structure for broader clay-product and refractory manufacturing, while NIST documents manufacturing AI uses in inspection, maintenance, forecasting, and process decisions. I map higher exposure to estimating, order and specification processing, scheduling, purchasing, documentation, kiln analytics, and vision inspection; technical and supervisory work is partly exposed; quarry/material handling, forming, kiln loading, firing, finishing, testing, packing, and maintenance execution receive little exposure. Broader-industry evidence may overstate exposure in small brick plants and understate it in instrumented refractory operations.
caveats: BLS occupation evidence is broader than the six-digit population.
Energy, yield, and scrap benefits are not labor substitution unless they remove labor hours or avoid hiring.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task census across 12-15 independent refractory, brick, and tile plants, including time studies for quoting, kiln control, inspection, setup, packing, and maintenance, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: Process monitoring and defect recognition create real analytic opportunity, but most direct work moves, forms, fires, and handles heavy physical materials.

d5.bridge: Census defines a mix of brick, structural and ceramic tile, and refractories used in furnace linings. Census reports construction spending for the first five months of 2026 was $858.4 billion, 2.7% below the same 2025 period, while commercial refractory evidence identifies steel, cement, glass, and ceramics as principal demand sectors and reports 934.3 million tonnes of global crude steel production in the first half of 2025. I use these only as cyclical and end-market context, then estimate constant-price US five-year demand across the current basket. Global steel and nominal construction measures can overstate or understate domestic real unit demand.
caveats: Construction spending is nominal and a short-term cycle indicator, not a five-year quantity forecast.
The steel statistic is global and refractory demand also depends on process intensity and relining frequency.
Building materials and refractories can move in different cycles.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted Census product-class shipments, US brick and tile unit shipments, refractory tons sold by end market, furnace relining intervals, and US steel, cement, glass, and construction forecasts would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Refractories are consumed and replaced wherever high-temperature industrial processes continue, while brick and tile follow construction, renovation, and infrastructure. Cyclicality, imports, alternative building materials, furnace efficiency, and lower material intensity restrain growth.

caveats: The provided count is estimated using a broad sector margin and may misclassify the EBITDA band.
Census defines establishments while transfer eligibility applies to firms that may own multiple plants.
e.change_evidence: Screen all 129 estimated in-band firms for captive status, repeat external revenue, ownership, customer and end-market concentration, permits, normalized EBITDA, and transferable mineral/formulation rights using commercial data and direct outreach.
e.rationale: Most in-band operating firms repeatedly supply external industrial, construction, distribution, or government customers and have transferable plants, formulations, mineral sources, product approvals, molds, and customer relationships. Captive units, distress, owner-held know-how, environmental liabilities, customer concentration, and firms misclassified by the margin bridge reduce eligibility.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 327120 ($1,514,351,148) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 327120 ($6,643,484,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1465 (margins.json[32]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Paper/Forest Products EBITDA/Sales 14.65% (mid-range for wood/paper/plastics)); revenue/employee $285,643 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 327120); class <5: 82 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,639,085 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $240,126 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 45 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,259,800 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $184,561 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 39 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,051,641 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $447,065 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 84 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,040,810 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,910,479 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 45 firms, avg revenue/firm $37,111,267 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,436,801 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 29 firms, avg revenue/firm $123,032,379 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $18,024,244 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 129

caveats: An operator can remain necessary while production consolidates or shifts offshore.
Longer-lasting refractories can reduce replacement quantity without eliminating operator responsibility.
o.change_evidence: Engineering interviews with steel, cement, glass, foundry, architect, contractor, and distributor buyers plus bill-of-material and relining histories would quantify material substitution, service-life extension, and insourcing.
o.rationale: The current basket consists of physical structural and heat-resistant products that must be formulated, shaped, fired or hardened, tested, and delivered by accountable operators. Software cannot line a furnace or supply brick and tile; limited loss can come from alternative materials, longer refractory life, redesigned furnaces or buildings, and customer insourcing.

caveats: Capture differs sharply between recurring engineered refractories and bid-driven commodity brick or tile.
No representative contract dataset was found.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts and five-year product/project histories for price, rebates, energy and raw-material surcharges, freight, volume, warranty, and gross margin across refractory and building-material suppliers would reveal pass-through.
q.rationale: Refractory formulations, service performance, qualification, local freight, color and architectural matching, and approved specifications can preserve savings. Steel, cement, glass, distributor, contractor, and project buyers still use bids, annual negotiations, material and energy indexing, and alternate suppliers to reclaim benefits over five years.

rho.bridge: NIST reports 46% of manufacturers using AI tools and more than 80% expecting to increase use within two years, while listing data quality, initial cost, skills, cybersecurity, and legacy integration as barriers. I translate those broad adoption signals into the five-year implementation share of exposed work. Chatbot inclusion overstates operational use today; a five-year horizon allows diffusion of packaged vision, planning, and maintenance tools.
caveats: General manufacturing adoption is not six-digit implementation evidence.
Safety and quality accountability constrain autonomous action in kilns and refractory formulations.
rho.change_evidence: Comparable-plant project records showing baseline labor, sensor coverage, integration cost, false-reject rates, validation, uptime, and sustained hours saved would materially move the estimate.
rho.rationale: Commercial and planning tools can deploy relatively quickly, but harsh high-temperature environments, old kilns, sensor gaps, product variability, customer specifications, and small-company engineering resources constrain operational deployment.

s5.bridge: Deloitte surveyed 500 US current- and next-generation family-enterprise members and found only 24% of current-generation respondents strongly agreed their succession plan could withstand departure of an important family employee. I treat this as broad succession-pressure evidence and reduce potential exits for family succession, non-control transactions, closures, and failed sales. The survey is not industry-specific or a timing measure and may include larger, better-advised businesses.
caveats: No six-digit qualifying-transfer denominator was found.
Succession fragility is not an observed transaction probability.
s5.change_evidence: Owner-age and intended-exit outreach to 327120 firms plus five years of closed control deals from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, building-material brokers, and refractory-industry advisers would narrow the range.
s5.rationale: Independent regional building-material and specialty-refractory producers can face owner succession, but capital intensity, remediation exposure, quarry or mineral rights, and customer qualification can prevent a qualifying five-year transfer.

principal_driver: Essential physical heat-resistant and structural products create recurring operator-required demand.
principal_weakness: Heavy physical production and cyclical, procurement-led end markets constrain labor substitution and retained benefit.
